document_name: Safety Management Requirements for Defence Systems

nodes.title: Safety Case
nodes.summary: A structured safety argument supported by evidence (drawn from the Information Set) demonstrating a PSS is safe for a defined application and environment. It is a central deliverable referenced across safety reporting and change management activities.

nodes.title: Safety Management
nodes.summary: The management discipline for planning, coordinating, and controlling safety activities across the contract and lifecycle. It frames how safety engineering, reporting, and governance are organised within the standard.

nodes.title: Safety Engineering
nodes.summary: The engineering activities used to design, analyse, and implement safety measures and to generate evidence for safety requirement satisfaction. It is a core pillar alongside safety management and in-service safety.

nodes.title: Safety In-Service
nodes.summary: Safety activities and controls applied during operation and support, including monitoring, reporting, and remedial action. It connects safety assurance to in-service data and ongoing risk management.

nodes.title: Safety Management Plan
nodes.summary: The plan defining how safety will be achieved, assured, and managed, including processes for analysis, reporting, and deliverables such as ISSS and Safety Case. It is a contractual control point requiring definition, agreement, and update with UK MOD.

nodes.title: Information Set
nodes.summary: The controlled body of safety-relevant information that must be defined, maintained, preserved, and made accessible across the contract. It is the primary evidence source for the Safety Case and supports audits, reporting, and change management.

nodes.title: Information Set Safety Summary
nodes.summary: A safety reporting deliverable produced from the Information Set to provide stakeholders (e.g., integrators and operators) with sufficient safety information, assumptions, and limitations. It is delivered incrementally to provide visibility of safety engineering and analysis progress.

nodes.title: Hazard Analysis
nodes.summary: Systematic analysis used to identify hazards and potential accidents and to inform safety requirements, design for safety, and safety case evidence. It explicitly includes considerations such as human factors and cyber security where relevant.

nodes.title: Hazard Log
nodes.summary: The record used to track hazards, controls, and related safety requirements, providing visibility of hazard control status throughout the contract. It supports reporting (Hazard Log Reports) and links to requirements management and evidence.

nodes.title: Safety Requirements
nodes.summary: Requirements derived from hazard analysis and other safety engineering activities that must be identified, documented, tracked, validated, and evidenced as satisfied. They are managed with bi-directional traceability and are linked into the Hazard Log and Safety Case evidence.

nodes.title: Derived Safety Requirement
nodes.summary: A safety requirement derived during design and analysis activities and requiring the same rigor of tracking, evidence, and satisfaction as other safety requirements. It is explicitly included in satisfaction and evidence obligations and is managed through the SMP and traceability processes.

nodes.title: Design for Safety
nodes.summary: The design activity that implements and manages mitigation strategies to meet safety requirements and to address shortfalls, feeding evidence into the Safety Case. It includes identifying, implementing, and assessing the effectiveness of mitigations and allocating derived requirements.

nodes.title: Safety Analysis
nodes.summary: Analytical activities conducted to support hazard understanding, validate design decisions, and generate evidence consistent with the evolving design. Results are documented and used to support safety arguments and ongoing assurance.

nodes.title: Failure Mode
nodes.summary: A potential way a system or element can fail that may contribute to hazards and must be identified, assessed, and tracked with visible control status. Failure mode information and assumptions underpinning assessments must be recorded in the Information Set and reflected in ISSS where required.

nodes.title: Risk Estimation
nodes.summary: The process for estimating risk (including qualitative approaches where quantification is impracticable) and documenting the basis for estimates. It supports subsequent risk evaluation and informs decisions on residual risk and compliance.

nodes.title: Risk and Compliance Evaluation
nodes.summary: Evaluation of risk to life and compliance against criteria, with results recorded to support assurance and decision-making. It links risk estimation outputs to acceptance decisions and compliance evidence used in safety reporting and safety case arguments.

nodes.title: Safety Audits
nodes.summary: Audit activities and reporting used to assess safety management and implementation, including audit findings and remedial actions. The topic connects to independence expectations and access to the Information Set for assurance purposes.

nodes.title: Independent Safety Auditor
nodes.summary: An independent assurance role (ISA) that may be granted reasonable access to the Information Set to perform independent safety audit activities. Independence and access constraints are key to credible assurance and governance.

nodes.title: Contractor Safety Auditor
nodes.summary: The contractor’s safety auditor role (CSA) responsible for audit activities and reporting, with explicit requirements for independence. It supports the audit regime that underpins safety assurance and compliance evidence.

nodes.title: Change Control
nodes.summary: The controlled process for managing changes to PSS and associated safety artefacts, ensuring safety impacts are assessed and reflected in the Information Set and Safety Case. It is central to maintaining safety integrity during supply and in-service evolution.

nodes.title: ALARP principle
nodes.summary: The risk reduction principle 'As Low As Reasonably Practicable' used to frame acceptable risk decisions, particularly for risk to life. It is defined in the document and tied to risk management and assurance expectations.

nodes.title: Human Factors
nodes.summary: Human factors considerations must be incorporated into hazard analysis and failure mode assessment where they may contribute to causes or provide mitigation. It links safety engineering practice to the human-system interaction domain and to external standards on human factors integration.

nodes.title: Cyber Security Considerations
nodes.summary: Cyber security must be considered in hazard and failure mode analysis where unauthorised electronic interactions could contribute to hazards, and where controls may provide mitigation. It connects safety assurance to cyber standards and policy references used in defence supply.

nodes.title: Defence Standard 00-055
nodes.summary: A key referenced defence standard used throughout the document, including for programmable elements and systematic failures considerations. It provides authoritative external requirements that inform hazard analysis, safety requirements, and compliance evidence.

nodes.title: Def Stan 05-138 Cyber Security for Defence Suppliers
nodes.summary: A referenced cyber security standard used to support cyber considerations within hazard and risk activities. It anchors cyber-related safety analysis and risk estimation to recognised defence supplier requirements.

nodes.title: Def Stan 00-251 Human Factors Integration for Defence Systems
nodes.summary: A referenced standard providing authoritative guidance for integrating human factors into defence systems. It supports the document’s requirement to consider human factors in hazard and failure mode analysis.

nodes.title: IEC 61508 Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems
nodes.summary: An external functional safety standard referenced as informative guidance for safety-related E/E/PE systems. It provides a widely recognised baseline for safety lifecycle and integrity concepts relevant to defence safety engineering.

nodes.title: POSMS
nodes.summary: A referenced MOD safety management system and associated procedures used as supporting guidance for safety management planning and policy/legislation agreement. It provides an external framework that complements the standard’s safety management requirements.

nodes.title: Regulatory Article (RA) RA1200
nodes.summary: A referenced regulatory article used to support safety management system expectations, particularly in airworthiness contexts. It provides authoritative regulatory alignment for safety planning and assurance activities.

nodes.title: IET Code of Practice for Independent Safety Assessors (COPISA)
nodes.summary: An external code of practice referenced to support independence expectations for safety auditors/assessors. It underpins credibility of independent safety assurance activities described in the audit sections.

nodes.title: STPA Handbook
nodes.summary: A referenced handbook for System Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA), supporting systematic hazard analysis approaches. It provides an external method reference for hazard identification and analysis rigor.

nodes.title: UK Health and Safety Executive (HSE)
nodes.summary: The UK regulator referenced for general health and safety guidance and terminology. It anchors the document’s health and safety framing and supports alignment with UK legal expectations.

nodes.title: Joint Service Publication (JSP) 815
nodes.summary: A referenced MOD publication providing policy/guidance context for safety management. It supports the document’s alignment with MOD safety governance and expectations.

nodes.title: JSP 822
nodes.summary: A referenced MOD publication used in the risk management section to support managing risk to life and related governance. It provides external policy context for risk management decisions and responsibilities.

nodes.title: Def Stan 05-057 Configuration Management of Defence Materiel
nodes.summary: A referenced configuration management standard supporting information consistency and change control across safety artefacts. It underpins requirements for managing the Information Set and build/change processes.
